title: AirPAY vs Square: Fees, Hardware, Settlement, and POS Fit
metaTitle: AirPAY vs Square: Fees, Hardware, Settlement, and POS Fit
description: Compare AirPAY and Square across in-person fees, fixed costs, hardware, settlement, POS integration, and online payment expansion.
lead: AirPAY and Square both allow Japanese merchants to begin cashless acceptance with limited fixed software cost, but their strengths differ. AirPAY emphasizes broad local payment coverage and AirREGI integration, while Square emphasizes published hardware, unified POS, and expansion into online commerce.
author: Luqevora.com Editorial Team

sources.label: Airペイ — 決済手数料・振込手数料
Airペイ — 決済手数料ディスカウントプログラム
Airペイ — 入金サイクル
Airペイ — 対応している決済の種類

sections.heading: Verdict: choose around the existing workflow and future channels
body: AirPAY is a natural fit for an AirREGI store that values Japanese e-money and QR coverage and can operate with three or six settlements monthly.
Square is easier to understand when the business wants POS and payments from one provider and expects to add online selling, invoices, or appointments.

sections.heading: Fee comparison: do not decide from the minimum headline
body: AirPAY's 2.48% and Square's 2.5% rates each have eligibility conditions. Other brands, e-money, QR, online, and manually entered transactions can use different rates, so a weighted sales model is required.
A zero monthly fee does not eliminate iPad, reader, terminal, printer, connectivity, or industry-software cost. Compare a three-year total.

rows: Standard payment and POS software at JPY 0
2.5% for eligible merchants and brands; 3.25% or higher otherwise
Compatible iPad or iPhone plus reader and peripherals
Reader, Stand, Terminal, and other published hardware options
Three or six deposits monthly depending on bank
Next business day for two named banks; weekly for other supported banks
Square POS in the same ecosystem
Separate online function and settlement rules
Online store, invoices, and payment links under one account

sections.heading: Settlement and working capital
body: AirPAY deposits six times monthly for selected major banks and three times monthly for other supported banks. Square documents next-business-day deposits for two named banks and weekly Friday deposits for other supported banks.
A business with short supplier or payroll cycles may value settlement speed more than a small processing-rate difference. Model the maximum outstanding balance.

sections.heading: Hardware and counter design
body: AirPAY combines a compatible Apple device and reader and can transfer the amount from AirREGI, helping unify checkout for an AirREGI user.
Square offers a small reader, a stand, and a printer-equipped terminal, with published prices that simplify the initial hardware budget.

sections.heading: Best-fit stores
body: AirPAY fits AirREGI users, stores emphasizing domestic e-money and QR methods, and Apple-device operations.
Square fits businesses that want to integrate POS, payments, ecommerce, and invoices over time or select dedicated hardware with published pricing.

sections.heading: Build a comparable model
body: Segment the last three months of sales by payment method, apply the relevant rate, and add devices, tablets, printers, subscriptions, paid features, and the cash-flow effect of settlement timing.

faqs.question: Should I simply choose the lowest processing rate?
faqs.answer: No. Compare the effective cost including eligibility, payment mix, hardware, subscriptions, settlement, and integration.

faqs.question: Should POS and payments come from one provider?
faqs.answer: One provider can simplify amount transfer and support, but also compare functionality, rates, and portability.

faqs.question: Which sales data is needed for a comparison?
faqs.answer: Prepare at least three months of value and transaction count by card brand, e-money, QR, cash, and in-person or online channel.
